title: Fast-forward and three-way merge

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Fast-forward and three-way merge; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Git Reference Documentation; current stable accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Fast-forward and three-way merge
sources.claim: A merge combines histories by finding ancestry and creating either a fast-forward reference move or a merge result that reconciles both lines of work.

sources.locator: Pro Git; second edition online; section applicable to Fast-forward and three-way merge
sources.claim: A merge combines histories by finding ancestry and creating either a fast-forward reference move or a merge result that reconciles both lines of work.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A merge combines histories by finding ancestry and creating either a fast-forward reference move or a merge result that reconciles both lines of work.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: When main has not advanced, merging feature fast-forwards main to the feature commit without creating an extra merge commit. Your goal is to perform and explain fast-forward and divergent merges in a disposable repository. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Commits and history; Short-lived feature branches. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A merge combines histories by finding ancestry and creating either a fast-forward reference move or a merge result that reconciles both lines of work. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Merge strategy, rename detection, binary files, generated artifacts, and semantic interactions can produce a textually clean merge that still breaks behavior. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A merge is successful and correct whenever Git reports no textual conflicts.

includes: Predict when Git moves a ref or creates a merge commit and choose a repository policy deliberately.
Perform and explain fast-forward and divergent merges in a disposable repository.
excludes: Merge strategy, rename detection, binary files, generated artifacts, and semantic interactions can produce a textually clean merge that still breaks behavior.

outcomes: Perform and explain fast-forward and divergent merges in a disposable repository.

examples.title: Fast-forward and three-way merge: direct case
examples.situation: When main has not advanced, merging feature fast-forwards main to the feature commit without creating an extra merge commit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: When main has not advanced, merging feature fast-forwards main to the feature commit without creating an extra merge commit.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: After reconciling history, inspect the combined diff and run relevant builds and tests because semantic incompatibilities can remain without conflict markers.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because After reconciling history, inspect the combined diff and run relevant builds and tests because semantic incompatibilities can remain without conflict markers.

examples.title: Fast-forward and three-way merge: boundary case
examples.situation: When both branches advanced, Git performs a three-way merge and tests reveal an API interaction even though no text conflict occurred.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: When both branches advanced, Git performs a three-way merge and tests reveal an API interaction even though no text conflict occurred.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Merge strategy, rename detection, binary files, generated artifacts, and semantic interactions can produce a textually clean merge that still breaks behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A merge is successful and correct whenever Git reports no textual conflicts.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Perform and explain fast-forward and divergent merges in a disposable repository.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A merge is successful and correct whenever Git reports no textual conflicts.
misconceptions.correction: After reconciling history, inspect the combined diff and run relevant builds and tests because semantic incompatibilities can remain without conflict markers.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A merge is successful and correct whenever Git reports no textual conflicts.

analogy.example: A route map can model Fast-forward and three-way merge: movable signposts identify current destinations, routes diverge, and integration reconnects selected paths.
usefulFor: Visualizing references, divergence, ancestry, merge bases, and reconciled paths
limitations: Branches are lightweight references to commits, not copied roads or independent containers of files.
Merge strategy, rename detection, binary files, generated artifacts, and semantic interactions can produce a textually clean merge that still breaks behavior.

After reconciling history, inspect the combined diff and run relevant builds and tests because semantic incompatibilities can remain without conflict markers.

recap.summary: You should now be able to perform and explain fast-forward and divergent merges in a disposable repository. Keep this boundary in view: Merge strategy, rename detection, binary files, generated artifacts, and semantic interactions can produce a textually clean merge that still breaks behavior.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Fast-forward and three-way merge without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.

Apply the model to this case from memory: When both branches advanced, Git performs a three-way merge and tests reveal an API interaction even though no text conflict occurred.
